core_intuitions: SPMD: every GPU runs one process running the same program on different data, branching only by its integer rank.
The six collectives differ purely in their dataflow, and the master identity is All-Reduce = Reduce-Scatter then All-Gather (the basis of ZeRO/FSDP).
Ring all-reduce is bandwidth-optimal: each rank moves ~2M bytes regardless of n, because reduce-scatter + all-gather each send (n-1)/n*M in equal M/n steps around the ring.

The alpha-beta cost model T = alpha + B/beta splits time into a fixed latency floor and a bandwidth-limited slope, so small messages are latency-bound and large messages bandwidth-bound; the ring latency floor 2(n-1)alpha grows with n, which is why we bucket small gradients and go hierarchical.

The interconnect is a steep hierarchy: NVSwitch gives full non-blocking all-to-all NVLink (~10-50x faster) inside a node while InfiniBand is the slow inter-node hop, so production collectives are hierarchical (reduce on NVLink, exchange once over IB, broadcast back).

Each parallelism strategy is defined by its collective: DDP=all-reduce, ZeRO/FSDP=reduce-scatter+all-gather, tensor-parallel=all-reduce in a sub-group, MoE/expert-parallel=all-to-all (a data transpose across ranks).

existing.name: collectives-six-dataflow
existing.issue: The name and marker promise all six collectives and the chapter explicitly calls them 'six operations you need to know cold', but the figure only draws three (All-Reduce, Reduce-Scatter, All-Gather). Broadcast, Reduce, and All-to-All - the latter being the whole basis of the MoE section later - are missing, so a reader mapping the six-row table to the picture comes up short.
existing.improvement: Add three compact panels for Broadcast (rank 0 buffer fans out to all), Reduce (all buffers funnel to rank 0 only), and All-to-All (rank i's j-th chunk lands as rank j's i-th chunk - draw it as a grid transpose so the distinct-chunk-to-each-rank pattern is unmistakable). Keep the existing A/B/C decomposition panels and the 'All-Reduce = Reduce-Scatter + All-Gather' footer as the punchline. Nudge the 'all-reduce (SUM)' / 'reduce-scatter (SUM)' labels off the arrow shafts so they don't overprint in dark mode.

existing.name: ring-allreduce
existing.improvement: Strong in both themes: the two-phase story (reduce-scatter then all-gather), the per-edge 'send X' labels, the per-rank owned-chunk result, and the cost/rank footer converging to 2M all land cleanly. One low-risk QA check: confirm the all-gather cost term in the footer renders as (n-1)/n*M (the light-theme raster made the minus glyph look like a plus); the value is correct, just verify the glyph.

existing.name: ring-ar-reduce-scatter-trace
existing.issue: Dark-mode legibility fails: the rank cards, the per-step log boxes, and the bottom banner are filled with a hardcoded dark-navy that nearly merges with the dark page background, and the gray body text inside them drops to very low contrast - the step log and rank results are hard to read. The panel is also dense (ring + side step-log + bottom banner all competing).
existing.improvement: Make the panel fills, borders, and text theme-aware so the navy cards read clearly on a dark page (lighter fill or brighter border+text). While in there, lighten the visual load: the side step-log repeats what the ring already shows, so trim it to just the accumulation state per step and let the ring carry the motion. Keep it a static trace - ring-allreduce already covers the mechanism, this one earns its place only if the per-step accumulation is crisply legible.

existing.name: nvswitch-crossbar-topology
existing.issue: The core diagram (8 GPUs, crossbar, bandwidth callouts) is clear, but in dark mode the title ('DGX H100 Intra-Node Topology...'), the two-line bottom caption, and the 'One-shot reduce across fabric' annotation box use a dark-gray text color that is near-invisible on the dark background - exactly the takeaway sentences are the ones that vanish.
existing.improvement: Switch the title, bottom caption, and annotation-box text to a theme-aware foreground color so they stay legible on both backgrounds. Leave the GPU boxes, purple crossbar, and green bandwidth panel as-is (they read fine). Optionally tone the solid purple crossbar toward a theme-neutral accent, but that is secondary to fixing the text contrast.

existing.name: hierarchical-allreduce-stages
existing.issue: Excellent three-stage narrative (intra-node reduce-scatter on NVLink -> single inter-node all-reduce on IB -> intra-node all-gather on NVLink) and the 'NVLink steps wrap the one expensive IB step' point is exactly right. But the title and the two-line bottom caption render in dark-gray and are almost unreadable in dark mode, so the summarizing sentence - the whole moral of the figure - disappears.
existing.improvement: Make the title and bottom-caption text theme-aware (foreground color that has contrast on both light and dark). The stage panels are light-filled so their interior labels survive; only the on-background text needs fixing. Consider bumping the 'bottleneck' label on the IB stage to a slightly stronger weight so the reason for the hierarchy pops.

new_figures.name: alpha-beta-cost-model
new_figures.concept: Plots T(B) = alpha + B/beta to show the latency floor vs the bandwidth slope, the latency-bound/bandwidth-bound crossover, and why the ring latency floor rises with n.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's stated purpose is teaching 'the exact cost models engineers use to reason about whether a training job will be network-bound or compute-bound', yet the alpha-beta model - the single most reused quantitative tool in the chapter and the basis of the bucketing, hierarchical, and interview-corner arguments - has no figure. One line chart makes alpha (fixed startup), 1/beta (asymptotic rate), the two regimes, and the n-dependence of the latency floor all click at once. This is the biggest genuine gap.
new_figures.brief: A single clean line chart. X-axis: message size B (small on the left, large on the right). Y-axis: transfer time T. Draw the line T = alpha + B/beta: a horizontal dashed segment at height alpha near the origin labeled 'latency floor alpha (fixed startup, per call)', then the line rising with a constant slope labeled 'slope = 1/beta (bandwidth-limited)'. Shade/annotate two regions under the curve: left region 'latency-bound: T ~ alpha, message too small to amortize startup', right region 'bandwidth-bound: T ~ B/beta, transfer dominated by raw bytes'. Mark the crossover point where alpha = B/beta with a vertical guide labeled 'B* = alpha*beta (equal cost)'. Add a small annotation arrow near the left region reading 'gradient bucketing merges many tiny messages -> pushes right into the bandwidth regime'. Then an inset (or a second faint pair of parallel lines on the same axes) showing the ring all-reduce variant T = 2(n-1)alpha + (2(n-1)/n)(M/beta): draw two or three lines for increasing n, same slope but each with a HIGHER intercept, labeled 'ring latency floor 2(n-1)alpha grows with n -> why we go hierarchical at large n'. Use only small illustrative integers for n (e.g. n=4, 16, 64) and NO absolute bandwidth/latency numbers - the axes are qualitative. Theme-safe: no hardcoded primary fills, use theme foreground for text and a neutral accent for the shaded regions; everything inside the viewBox with clear reading order left-to-right.

new_figures.name: all-to-all-moe-dispatch
new_figures.concept: Shows MoE token routing as an all-to-all: tokens on each GPU are colored by their destination expert, and the all-to-all shuffle delivers every token to the GPU that hosts its expert.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter devotes a full section and code sketch to all-to-all for expert parallelism but has zero visuals for it, and none of the existing figures draw all-to-all's distinctive dataflow (each rank sends a distinct chunk to every other rank - a data transpose). Seeing tokens sorted by color and transposed across GPUs is the moment MoE routing clicks and the 'bisection-bandwidth-limited, no ring benefit' cost intuition lands. Foundational to the DP/TP/EP-uses-which-collective mapping the chapter closes on.
new_figures.brief: Two-column before/after over a small illustrative n (e.g. 4 GPUs, one expert per GPU). BEFORE (left): four GPU rows, each holding a short strip of token cells. Color each token cell by its DESTINATION expert (4 colors), scattered - so every GPU has a mix of all four colors, representing a router that sent tokens everywhere. Label 'each GPU holds tokens bound for many different experts'. MIDDLE: the all-to-all operation drawn as a crossing/transpose bundle of arrows - make clear that GPU i sends its color-j tokens to GPU j and receives color-i tokens from everyone (a grid transpose). Small caption 'all-to-all = transpose: rank i's chunk-for-j lands on rank j'. AFTER (right): four GPU rows again, but now each GPU's strip is a single solid color - GPU j holds exactly the tokens for expert j, ready for local expert compute. Label 'each GPU now holds only its own expert's tokens'. Footer line: 'cost ~ O(total token volume), limited by network bisection bandwidth - unlike all-reduce it gets no ring speedup'. Keep counts as small illustrative integers, invent no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: distinguish the four experts with a categorical, theme-neutral palette (not brand primaries), keep all labels in theme foreground, fit within the viewBox with an obvious left-to-right reading order.

summary: The chapter is figure-rich and mostly well-served: the ring all-reduce mechanism and the NVLink/NVSwitch/IB hierarchy each have strong figures. Two things drag on the existing set. First, four of the five figures render fine in light mode but leak dark-gray title/caption text or hardcoded navy panels that go near-invisible in dark mode (ring-ar trace worst, plus the two topology figures' summarizing captions) - all theme-safety refines, not redraws. Second, the 'six-dataflow' figure only draws three collectives despite the chapter enumerating six, so it needs Broadcast/Reduce/All-to-All added. The real conceptual gaps are two: the alpha-beta cost model (the chapter's central quantitative tool, currently unillustrated) deserves the one must-have new figure, and the all-to-all/MoE section has a whole story with no visual. ring-allreduce is a clean keep.
